one.zh: HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS ORDER – Where the Court placed the parties on notice of its intention to consider making an order under s 102QAC of the Family Law…
one.en: HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS ORDER – Where the Court placed the parties on notice of its intention to consider making an order under s 102QAC of the Family Law…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS ORDER – Where the Court placed the parties on notice of its intention to consider making an order under s 102QAC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) – Where the respondent sought a harmful proceedings order against the applicant – Where the applicant declined the opportunity to advance a formal position in relation to the exercise of the Court’s authority under the Act – Where the applicant’s conduct and constant filing of applications in this Court, and in other jurisdictions, caused the Court to find that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the respondent and the children would suffer ‘harm’ if the respondent was allowed to instituted further proceedings without leave of the Court – Where the Court makes an order under s 102QAC of the Act – Where the respondent’s position was that he wished to be notified in the event of the applicant seeking to file any further application – Where the respondent also sought to be notified by the Court where any application sought to be filed by the applicant was dismissed by the Court – Where the Court made orders in the terms sought by the respondent. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the respondent sought an order that the applicant pay his costs incurred, on an indemnity basis, of and incidental to four applications commenced and later discontinued by the applicant – Where the applicant was ordered to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding, and to file necessary accompanying documents in relation to the respondent’s application for costs – Where the applicant elected not to comply with the Orders of the Court – Where the applicant failed to attend the hearing– Where the Court made an order for the applicant to pay the respondent’s costs on an indemnity basis.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These reasons for judgment explain the orders made by this Court on 28 November 2025 as between the Applicant Mother (“the mother”) and the Respondent Father (“the father”) (collectively “the parents”) concerning proceedings commenced, and later discontinued, in relation to their children, X born 2009 and Y born 2012 (collectively “the children”).

BRIEF BACKGROUND

The mother was born in the United Kingdom, is forty-six years old and works as a professional. At the time of the hearing subject to these reasons for judgment, the mother was in the United Kingdom but habitually resides in Suburb B. It is unclear if the mother has re-partnered. The father was born in New Zealand, is forty-nine years old and works as a professional. The father and the children reside in rental accommodation in Suburb B. The father has re-partnered with Ms C, and this relationship commenced in January 2023. The parents met in late 2004, married in 2008 and separated on a final basis in November 2021.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

Regrettably, the parents have had long and arduous litigation experience in this Court. There have been no less than fifty orders made in these proceedings to date. By virtue of the length and complexity of these proceedings, these reasons for judgment will only traverse materially relevant orders and determinations.
Litigation commenced in June 2022 in relation to both property and parenting matters. Final orders were made by Judicial Registrar Bardetta on 19 July 2022, by consent, which provided for the children to spend time with the parents on an equal time basis. The orders of 19 July 2022 note that the parents also submitted to final property orders, however these were subject to requisition of the Registrar.
The orders of Judicial Registrar Bardetta on 20 September 2022, note that the mother made an oral application to have the parenting orders set aside on the ground of fraud and/or duress. According to notation one of the 20 September 2022 orders, the Judicial Registrar recorded that the mother orally argued that the consent orders made 19 July 2022 were not in the best interests of the children. Curiously, however, the mother did not allege that there had been any contravention of the consent orders nor a significant change in circumstances following the making of the orders. Judicial Registrar Bardetta dismissed the mother’s oral application to suspend or set aside the final parenting orders.
On 29 March 2023, the father filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking to suspend the orders made on 19 July 2022 in relation to parental responsibility, spend time arrangements and communication between the children and the mother. Additionally, the father sought sole parental responsibility, orders for the children to live with him, for the children to commence attendance upon a psychologist, an Independent Children’s Lawyer be appointed and for a Single Expert Report to be obtained.
On 30 March 2023, orders were made by Deputy Registrar Mowat urgently listing the father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 29 March 2023 for a direction hearing and requiring the mother to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding. The mother did not file a response in accordance with the orders of the Court.
On 18 April 2024, Judicial Registrar Bardetta made orders, once again, requiring the mother to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding and listed the matter for an interim hearing. Orders were also made appointing an Independent Children’s Lawyer and ordering a Family Report under s 62 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
On 16 June 2023, orders were made by consent by Judicial Registrar Bardetta in relation to the property proceedings. These orders were later amended pursuant to r 10.13 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Rules 2021 (Cth) on 27 June 2023 (“the June 2023 property orders”).
The matter came before her Honour Judge Neville for an interim hearing on 19 July 2023. Her Honour made orders which required the parents to facilitate the children engaging with ‘[D Family Services]’ for counselling and for the appointment of a psychiatrist to prepare a Single Expert Report. Judgment was otherwise reserved from this interim hearing.
On 22 August 2023, the father filed an Application – Enforcement in relation to the final property orders made by consent on 16 June 2023.
On 29 August 2023, her Honour Judge Neville delivered her decision from the interim hearing which occurred on 19 July 2023. Her Honour made orders which suspended orders 4 -11 of the Orders made 19 July 2022 which related to parental responsibility and spend time arrangements for the children. Further, orders were made for the children to live with the father, for the children to have passports issued or renewed without the consent of the mother, for the children to travel to New Zealand for two weeks, and restraint was placed on the mother from attending the children’s school and required her to continue her attendance upon her psychologist.
On 5 September 2023, the Court listed the matter for case management of the Application – Enforcement filed 22 August 2023 by the father. There was no appearance by or on behalf of the mother on this occasion, despite the Court being satisfied that she was aware of the listing and the matter being stood down and the legal representation being called.
On 27 September 2023, Judicial Registrar Bardetta made orders by consent which amended the June 2023 property orders. These orders resolved the Application – Enforcement filed 22 August 2023 and became the final property orders for the parents.
In November 2023, Deputy Registrar Ekert made a divorce order.
On 2 December 2023, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding. The content of this application largely sought to appeal and/or reagitate interim parenting issues which were heard and determined by her Honour Judge Neville on 29 August 2023 and to which interim orders issued thereafter.
On 13 December 2023, the father filed an Amended Application for Final Orders which sought to set aside and reagitate the final property orders made by consent on 27 September 2023.
On 15 December 2023, the father filed an Application – Contempt which alleged that the mother contravened s 121 of the Act.
On 14 February 2024, orders were made by consent by her Honour Judge Howe which resolved the Application – Contempt filed on 15 December 2023 by the father. The orders made restrained the mother from publishing on social media, or by any other means, any account of these proceedings which identifies a party, a person associated with the proceedings or a witness in the proceedings.
On 9 April 2024, her Honour Judge Stewart (as her Honour then was) transferred the proceedings to Division 1 of this Court. During the Court event, her Honour noted that the mother “demonstrated either an unwillingness or an inability to join the meeting” (Order made 9 April 2024, notation A).
On 24 April 2024, the matter came before his Honour Campton J and orders were made bifurcating the parenting and property proceedings. The parenting proceedings were set down for final hearing for three days before his Honour Schonell J. An order pursuant to s 102NA of the Act was made. His Honour noted that the mother did not attend the listing.
On 18 June 2024, the first day of the final hearing for the parenting proceedings before Schonell J, the matter settled by consent. His Honour made orders which provide for the parents to have equal shared parental responsibility, for the children to live with the father and to spend time with the mother in accordance with their wishes. Further, the mother was restrained from attending the children’s school without the father’s consent (“the Final Parenting orders”).
On 12 August 2024, orders were made his Honour Schonell J by consent for the father’s Further Amended Application filed 27 February 2024 to be withdrawn and dismissed. At this point in time all applications before the Court had been finalised.
On 28 November 2024, the mother filed an Application – Contravention which sought to discharge and reagitate the matters subject to the Final Parenting orders.
On 11 June 2025, her Honour Judge Obradovic transferred the proceedings back to Division 1 of this Court.
On 20 June 2025, the matter came before this Court for mention. I ordered a Child Impact Report and listed the matter for further mention on 30 September 2025.
On 29 May 2025, the mother filed a Notice of Objection – Subpoena in relation to the father’s request to obtain the Local Court file. On 1 September 2025, I listed this for hearing before Senior Judicial Registrar McDonald on 24 September 2025.
On 9 September 2025, the mother filed a further Notice of Objection – Subpoena in relation to a Subpoena to the Australia Federal Police issued by the father. This was also listed for determination on 24 September 2025.
On 15 September 2025, the mother filed an Application – Contempt which did not attach any details of her alleged contempt. This application was listed for mention before me on 30 September 2025.
On 24 September 2025, Senior Judicial Registrar dismissed the mother’s Notice of Objection – Subpoena filed 29 August 2025 and 9 September 2025.
On 26 September 2025, I released the Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert, Ms E dated 25 September 2025 (“the Child Impact Report”) to the father and the mother subject to being in the presence of a Family Advocacy and Support Service (“FASS”) worker.
On 30 September 2024, I made orders listing the Application – Contravention filed 28 November 2024 and the Application – Contempt filed on 15 September 2025 for hearing in relation to whether the Court should summarily dismiss both applications. On the Court’s own motion, the parties were advised that a harmful proceedings order would be considered on the next occasion. Pending further order, I restrained the mother pursuant to s 68B of the Act from initiating any communication with the children personally or by way of any third party.
On 3 October 2025, the mother filed a Notice of Discontinuance in relation to her Application – Contravention filed 28 November 2024, Application in a Proceeding filed 16 June 2025, and her Application – Contempt filed 15 September 2025.
On 17 October 2025, the mother filed a Notice of Discontinuance in relation to her Initiating Application filed 16 June 2025. This resolved all applications filed by the mother in this Court.
On 23 October 2025, Senior Judicial Registrar McDonald made an order that the mother was to pay the father’s costs of the subpoena hearing held on 24 October 2025 fixed in the amount of $1,500.
On 31 October 2025, the father filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking costs in relation to the mother’s Application – Contravention filed 28 November 2024, the Initiating Application and Application in a Proceeding filed 16 June 2025 and amended on 10 September 2025, and the Application – Contempt filed 13 September 2025 and amended 17 September 2025 (“the father’s costs application”). This is application was the only application on foot at the hearing, and it forms the subject to these reasons. The Court notes, however, that the consideration of making a harmful proceedings order remained a live issue during the hearing.
On 3 November 2025, I made orders requiring the mother to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding to the father’s costs application, a supporting affidavit and an updated financial statement. The mother did not do so.
On 28 November 2025, I made final orders and now provide these reasons.

THE EVIDENCE

In support of his orders sought, the father relied on the following evidence:
a) Written Submissions filed 27 November 2025 (“the father’s Written Submissions”);

b) His Affidavit filed 14 October 2025 including all annexures therein (“the father’s affidavit”);

c) Application in a Proceeding filed 31 October 2025 (“the father’s costs application”);

d) The father’s proposed minute of orders provided to Chambers on 28 November 2025;

e) The father’s proposed minute of orders handed up on 28 November 2025 (“the father’s proposal”);

f) Child Impact Report prepared by Court Child Expert, Ms C dated 25 September 2025 (“the Child Impact Report”); and

g) Various documents tendered during the hearing which became Exhibits A1-A2.

Despite being on notice that the Court would be considering making a harmful proceedings order pursuant to s 102QAC(1) of the Act, the mother did not rely on any evidence during the hearing, nor attend. The mother’s counsel informed the Court that the extent of his instructions was that the mother opposed a harmful proceedings order being made as well as, any costs order being made against her. The mother’s counsel merely attended the hearing as a courtesy to the Court.

THE PARTIES PROPOSALS

The father sought that a harmful proceedings order be made pursuant to s102QAC(1) of the Act and that pursuant to s 102QAC(2) of the Act the Court notify the father in the event that the mother made an application for leave to commence proceeding pursuant to s 102QAG of the Act. Additionally, the father sought an order for the mother to pay his costs of and incidental to the proceedings, either:
on an indemnity basis calculated at $59,600;
in a fixed sum of $35,000;
as assessed by the Court; or
at scale.

THE APPLICABLE LAW

Harmful Proceedings

The recent amendments to the Act have created a robust framework designed to curtail proceedings that are not only unmeritorious and/or vexatious but are harmful. The provisions found in Part XIB of the Act reflect a legislative shift towards providing safeguards to litigants and children from being subjected to the collateral damage that arises due to litigation.
Part XIB of the Act outlines the Court’s powers in dealing with proceedings that are “unmeritorious, harmful, or vexatious” (s 102QAA of the Act). In the Explanatory Memorandum to the Family Law Amendment Bill 2023 (Cth) at paragraph [309] (“the Explanatory Memorandum”), the rationale behind Division 1A and 1B of the Act was to “collocate all the Court’s powers for dealing with application or proceedings... to improve usability”.
The harmful proceedings provisions became operative from 6 May 2024. Parliament’s intention behind the harmful proceedings powers is found in the Explanatory Memorandum:
...The purpose of this measure is to protect the respondent and/or children who are the subject of proceedings from the harmful impact of frequent and unnecessary applications filed by an applicant. This measure aims to limit systems abuse, which is a form of family violence that is prevalent in the family law system.
This measure addresses a gap in the court’s powers to scrutinise the institution of further proceedings, cited in the case of Marsden & Winch [2013] FamCAFC 177; (2013) 50 Fam LR 409. The ALRC Report found that the court’s existing vexatious proceedings and summary dismissal powers do not provide sufficient scope for courts to make appropriate orders in cases where one party oppresses the other by repetitive filing of applications and the serving of those applications on the other party.
... The intention of this power is to allow the courts to proactively intervene, or intervene upon application by a party to the proceedings, before further applications are served on the other party, and therefore limit the detrimental effect, major mental distress or psychological harm that may result from further applications.
(Emphasis added).
S 102QAC of the Act provides, as follows:
102QAC Making harmful proceedings orders

Making harmful proceedings orders
A court exercising jurisdiction in proceedings under this Act may make an order (a harmful proceedings order prohibiting a party (the first party) to the proceedings from instituting proceedings under this Act against another party to the proceedings without the leave of the court under section 102QAG, if the court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that:
a) the other party would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party; or

b) in the case of child - related proceedings (within the meaning of Part VII)--the child who is the subject of the proceedings would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party.

For the purposes of subsection (1), harm may include, but is not limited to, the following:
a) psychological harm or oppression;

b) major mental distress;

c) a detrimental effect on the other party's capacity to care for a child;

d) financial harm.

In determining whether to make an order under subsection (1), the court may have regard to:
a) the history of the proceedings under this Act between the first party and the other party; and

b) whether the first party has frequently instituted or conducted proceedings against the other party in any Australian court or tribunal (including proceedings instituted (or attempted to be instituted) or conducted, and orders made, before the commencement of this section); and

c) the cumulative effect, or any potential cumulative effect, of any harm resulting from the proceedings referred to in paragraphs (a) and (b).

The court may make a harmful proceedings order on its own initiative or on application by a party to the proceedings.
The court must not make a harmful proceedings order in relation to a person without hearing the person or giving the person an opportunity of being heard.
An order made under subsection (1) is a final order.

Order about notifying other party in relation to application for leave etc.
If the court makes an order under subsection (1), the court must also make an order as to whether the court is to notify the other party, in the event that the first party makes an application under section 102QAE for leave to institute proceedings against the other party, of either or both of the following:
a) that the application has been made;

b) if the application is dismissed—that the application has been dismissed.

The court must have regard to the wishes of the other party in making an order under subsection (7).
A proper understanding of harm in the context of s 102QAC(2) of the Act is found in the non-exclusive, statutory components of harm.

Section 102QAC(2)(a) - psychological harm or oppression

These factors are expressed in the alternate; one or the other may suffice. According to the Macquarie Dictionary definition 1 ‘harm’ means to “damage or hurt” whilst ‘oppression’ means “the exercise of authority of power in a burdensome, cruel, or unjust manner”. In conjunction, the form of ‘harm’ intended to be addressed by both alternate propositions is limited to purely mental or emotional detriment induced by the imposition, or continuation of, purportedly harmful proceedings oppression. As the definition is inclusive, however, this might mean, for example, that physical and financial oppression is caught by this section.

Section 102QAC(2)(b) - major mental distress

According to the Macquarie Dictionary definition 1, ‘distress’ means “great pain, anxiety, or sorrow; acute suffering; affliction; trouble”. Mental distress, as a form of harm, is likely to arise from most proceedings under the Act. Justice Behrens noted in Cheadle & Pointer [2025] FedCFamC1F 27 at [84] that “much litigation will cause mental distress” and that “something more is required”. This may be surmised by reference to the statutory inclusion of the presupposition major mental distress in the Act. The form of harm sought to be addressed by this factor is extraordinary distress beyond the norm for the proceeding contemplated. Arguably the harm may be inferred.

Section 102QAC(2)(c) - a detrimental effect on the other party’s capacity to care for a child

Parental capacity is a well-accepted consideration in the determination of parenting orders that does not require explanation here. The inclusion of this factor demonstrates that harm may be indirect in the sense that the proceeding induces a net negative effect on a family unit via the degradation of core competencies of one or more parties. Further, it is important to note that this factor is not limited to adjudication of putative or current parenting proceedings. It may be such that auxiliary, contingent or bifurcated financial proceedings may have a harmful effect on a person’s parental capacity and thus enliven this sub-provision.

Section 102QAC(2)(d) - financial harm

According to the Macquarie Dictionary definition 1 ‘financial’ relates “to monetary receipts and expenditures”. Financial harm must therefore describe the detriment a person suffers through excess or unnecessary pecuniary loss as a result of pending or current proceedings that is beyond the norm similar claims under the Act.

Costs

The amendments to the Act brought into effect by the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) on 10 June 2025 apply to determine these competing applications. The power to order costs is conferred upon the Court by Part XIVC of the Act. Section 114UB of the Act is the central provision concerning costs orders and replaces the now repealed s 117 of the Act. Section 114UB is in materially the same terms as the repealed s 117. Neither party contended otherwise. I am satisfied that the body of authority decided under s 117 remains applicable.
The starting position is clear, that is, that each party should bear their own costs (s 114UB(1)). Subsection 114UB(2) provides that if the Court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, it may, subject to subsection (3), s 114UC and s 114UD and the applicable Rules, make any order for costs that the Court considers just. The discretion to award costs is a “broad” one, and the various enumerated factors are not to be read in any restrictive way (Collins and Collins [1985] FamCA 15; (1985) FLC 91-603).
When considering making a costs order, the Court must have regard to the following factors as outlined in subsection 114UB(3):
(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:
(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;
(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.
It is well settled that no single factor in s 114UB(3) has priority, nor must more than one factor be satisfied. Indeed, any one factor may be sufficient (Fitzgerald (as child representative for A (Legal Aid Commission of Tasmania)) v Fish and Anor [2005] FamCA 158; (2005) 33 Fam LR 123). However, the matters considered “must all be taken into account and all balanced in order to determine whether the overall circumstances justify the making of an order for costs” (I and I (No 2) (1995) FLC 92-625 (“I and I (No 2)”).

DISCUSSION

Harmful Proceedings order

Both subsections of s 102QAC(1) are relevant in this matter, such that the Court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe both the father and the children would suffer harm if the mother was to institute further proceedings in this Court against the father.

The harm experienced by the father

During the course of the father’s litigation history with the mother he has suffered financial harm and his capacity to care for the children has been affected. The father has been forced to meet various applications which were commenced and then later discontinued. During the course of the litigation, at times, the mother did not attend Court listings nor comply with orders of the Court for filing documents, even when such hearings arose out of applications she herself filed. Further, the mother consistently sent inappropriate correspondence to Chambers despite being informed numerous times of the guidelines for the same.
The Court Child Expert described the mother as “unreceptive to feedback” and has having “limited reflective capacity” (Child Impact Report, paragraph 42). The Court formed the same view of the mother based on her conduct throughout the proceedings.

The Airport Incident

A poignant example of the mother’s harmful conduct is seen in events surrounding the children attempting to travel with the father to F State for the Wedding of Ms C’s brother. The father sets out this series of events from paragraphs 70 –84 in his Affidavit (“the airport incident”). The Court does not intend to recount the father’s version of events verbatim, however, shall summarise. The Court pauses to note that Order 11 of the Final Parenting orders permitted the father to travel with the children internationally subject to the children’s wishes, and providing the mother with 14 days’ notice and details of the intended destination.
On 21 December 2024, the father informed the mother of his intention to travel to F State with the children in February 2025. On 28 January 2025, the mother requested that her solicitor have the children placed on the airport watchlist, as previously the children were on the airport watchlist during the contested substantive proceedings which had impeded her travel plans. On 4 February 2025, the mother retained a new legal representative who sought that the father urgently provides an itinerary and an undertaking for the intended travel, despite this not being required under the Final Parenting orders. The mother threatened to commence urgent proceedings should the father not comply with her request.
In February 2025, the father attended the airport with the children. The children and father were approached by security and escorted to a room where the Police assessed the situation. The father and his legal representatives both provided the Police with a copy of the Final Parenting orders and evidence of the father meeting the requirements as set out in the orders. After 2-3 hours of assessment, the Police informed the father that they would not be permitted to travel. The father described that “[Y] cried for the duration of the drive, and [X] was both devastated and angry” (Father’s Affidavit, paragraph 77). The Court accepts this evidence. as propounded by the father in relation to the airport incident based on the litigation history.
The father caused his legal representative to write to the mother’s legal representation in relation to the airport incident. The mother personally replied to the correspondence and raised that the children were unable to travel with her to Country G when they were on the airport watchlist (during the contested substantive proceedings), and threatened that should the father persist any further, she will claim “in the vicinity of $45,000.00” (father’s affidavit, paragraph 79).
The father filed a subpoena directed to the Australian Federal Police following the airport incident. The mother objected to this subpoena, and as outlined earlier in these reasons for judgment, her objection was dismissed, and she was ordered to pay the father’s costs. The subpoena material identified that the mother had sent the Australian Federal Police a handwritten airport watchlist application and claimed that the “Father has said he will leave Australia and never return” (father’s affidavit, paragraph 84.1). The father asserts that there is no basis for the mother to think this. The Court agrees. The father had provided the mother with details of both the children’s intended departure and return from F State. The material produced on subpoena also contained alleged text messages as between the mother and the children which purportedly conveyed that the travel was not in accordance with their wishes. The father does not believe that these text messages were sent by the children. This reflects a similar experience as described by X below. Additionally, an unfiled, unsealed and unsigned Initiating Application of the mother seeking airport watchlist orders was also produced under subpoena from the Australian Federal Police. The Court confirmed with the parents that no such Initiating Application was filed by the mother seeking to have the children placed on the watchlist.
The Court forms the view that the mother had no reasonable or legitimate concern that the children would not be returned to the Commonwealth of Australia, and rather, acted out of spite of the father. It is clear that the mother did not consider the impact on the children of being interviewed by the police and being held in an interview room for 2-3 hours, nor their extreme disappointment of not being permitted to travel. The mother’s rationale for her conduct in relation to the airport incident causes the Court great concern, as she would rather ensure that the children once again suffer by the inability to travel and cause the father the same financial harm, than allow the father to travel with the children.

Proceedings in other jurisdictions

The father identified examples of additional litigation instituted by the mother outside of this Court from paragraphs 88 – 90 of his affidavit. In short, the mother has threatened and engaged solicitors with respect to a potential civil liability claim against the father. The mother has also reported to the father to the police which resulted in him being charged, however the matter proceeded to a final hearing where the father was found not guilty and costs were made in his favour. Further, Applications for Family Violence Orders were filed against the father and Ms C for the protection of the mother and the children were filed in the Local Court by the mother. These applications were filed in June 2025 and July 2025, which is shortly after the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding with this Court which sought an equal time arrangement between the parents.
Further, the mother emailed the father to his work email address to advise she has reported him to the Australia Federal Police for the following:
Child sex offences by an Australian in a foreign country;
People smuggling;
Human trafficking, including:
Trafficking for slavery;
Trafficking or organ harvesting;
Trafficking for sexual, domestic and/or labour servitude; and
Forced marriage.

(father’s affidavit, paragraph 88.6).

The content raised in the mother’s emails to the father’s work email addressed breached his employer’s regulations and guidelines and this resulted in her email address being blocked on his work commuter. It also caused the father to have to report and explain his relationship dynamics to his superiors.
The father describes the mother’s conduct as having an “extremely negative impact”, causing “significant stress”, “extremely time consuming and stressful”, and “physically and mentally exhausting” having to defend numerous applications in various jurisdictions (father’s affidavit, paragraphs 92-96). The stress experienced by the father is exacerbated by also working full time and being the sole carer of the children.
The father expressed his concern to the Court Child Expert that his relationship with the children will be negatively affected if he continues to make attempts to force the children to see the mother contrary to their wishes, and further that it may jeopardise their sense of stability in his care (Child Impact Report, paragraph 32). Additionally, the father asserts that the children’s counsellor from D Family Services has notified him that he has “at times over-encouraged the children to spend time” with the mother and that this may cause “undue pressure” (father’s affidavit, paragraph 35). The Court shares the concern of the father and is satisfied that s 102QAC applies.
In compliance with s 102QAC(8) of the Act, the Court has considered the father’s preference to be notified of any application or dismissal thereof of any application the mother may make for leave under s 102QAG. There is no reason for the Court to not make orders in the form sought by the father. Accordingly, the Court made an order pursuant to s 102QAC(7).

The harm experienced by the children

At the outset the Court wishes to acknowledge that the children have been exposed to litigation in this Court since they were 12 and 9 years old respectively. They have been engaged with psychological intervention since her Honour Judge Neville ordered same on 19 July 2023. The father confirmed for the Court that the children attended upon D Family Services psychological support as well as the school counsellor consistently since the Final Parenting orders. These children have suffered as a consequence of the parent’s litigation. It is apposite to also acknowledge that during the course of these proceedings, the children have been made to interact with an Independent Children’s Lawyer, Court Child Expert Ms H, Psychiatrist Anthony Milch, Court Child Expert Ms C and the Australian Federal Police. The children require a safeguard to be protected from further psychological harm as a result of the mother’s frequent and unnecessary applications which seek to change the status quo.
Despite requiring the children to attend upon another professional, the Court was assisted by the Child Impact Report to consider the views of the children. It is noted that the children’s views expressed were consistent with the previously prepared Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert Ms H dated 8 June 2023 and the Single Joint Expert Report of Dr Milch dated 4 October 2023. It was contended by the mother at an earlier listing on 30 September 2025, that she spoke to the children after the Child Impact Report interviews and confirmed that the views expressed by the children in the Child Impact Report are now outdated. The Court does not accept this.
The eldest child, X described the mother as “unpredictable” and he appeared “visibly uncomfortable when discussing his relationship” with her (Child Impact Report, paragraph 13). X advised the Court Child Expert that he received “telephone contacts from his mother and maternal grandmother daily” despite not responding to the contact (Child Impact Report, paragraph 13). He described feeling “uncomfortable” when the mother and maternal grandmother appear at his house unannounced and attempt to get the children to return to the care of the mother (Child Impact Report, paragraph 14).
X recounted an experience whereby his mother gifted him a new phone, however he discovered that there was already text messages exchanged between the new phone and the mother’s phone number which claimed that he wished to live with the mother “forever”. X formed the view that the mother had sent these messages to herself, and he described this as “weird” (Child Impact Report, paragraph 14).
The child, Y described a similar experience with the mother and stated that she “pressures” him to stay at her home and he received excessive communication from her resulting in him having to block her phone number (Child Impact Report, paragraph 22). Unsurprisingly, Y advised the Court Child Expert that he feels as though the mother does not listen to him (Child Impact Report, paragraph 21). Of most concern to the Court is that Y “is worried about Court”.
The Court accepts that the children’s views expressed in the Child Impact Report are accurate and has taken them into consideration when evaluating the likelihood of them experiencing further harm as a result of any potential further applications being filed by the mother.
The father expressed his concern about the psychological harm that the children experience due to the persistence of the mother wanting them to live with her, which is reflected in her applications which have previously been before the Court (Child Impact Report, paragraph 32). The Court shares this concern.
The Court is satisfied that should the mother be permitted to file further applications without leave of the Court; the children would suffer further psychological harm.

Costs

The father sought an order that the mother pay his costs on an indemnity basis calculated to be $59,600 of an incidental to the following applications brought by the mother which were later discontinued:
Application – Contravention filed 28 November 2024;
Initiating Application/ Application in a Proceeding filed 16 June 2025 and amended 10 September 2025; and
Application – Contempt filed 13 September 2025 and amended 17 September 2025.
Where an order is sought for costs to be made on an indemnity basis, the Court must determine “whether the overall circumstances justify the making of an order for costs” (I and I (No 2) at 82,277) and “whether the particular facts and circumstances of the case in question warrant the making of an order for payment of costs other than on a party/party basis” (Colgate Palmolive Co v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; [1993] 46 FCR 225 at [257]).
The father appropriately addressed the considerations required by s 114UB(3) from paragraph 59 – 69 of his written submissions. The mother was required to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding in relation to the father’s costs application pursuant to Orders of this Court made 3 November 2025. The mother was provided with ample notice of the listing where such application would be considered. The mother declined to file any documents and elected to not attend the listing. Counsel who attended the listing on the mother’s behalf confirmed that he held no instructions other than to oppose the applications to be considered by the Court.
No argument was advanced that could suggest that a costs order on an indemnity basis was not entirely reasonable. The Court accepts the written submissions of the father. The Court independently assesses that, not only were the mother’s various applications completely unsuccessful and without merit, but her conduct, behaviour and disdain of the Court, and various third parties warrant the making of an order for indemnity costs. Even if the mother claimed impecuniosity, the Court would still make the order. The Court has considered the factors expressed in s 114UB(3) and is satisfied that an indemnity costs order is just in the circumstances of this matter.
